arguments.old_string:
/**
 * A record batch is a container for records. In old versions of the record format (versions 0 and 1),
 * a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain
 * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records
 * regardless of compression.
 */
public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable<Record> {
arguments.new_string:
/** * A record batch is a container for records in the Kafka protocol. In old versions of the record format * (versions 0 and 1), a batch consisted always of a single record if no compression was enabled, but could contain * many records otherwise. Newer versions (magic versions 2 and above) will generally contain many records * regardless of compression. * *

Role in Kafka Protocol: Record batches are the fundamental unit of serialization for Kafka message logs. * They encapsulate multiple records along with metadata such as offsets, timestamps, compression information, and * producer information (for idempotence and transactions). The batch structure allows for efficient storage and * transmission of multiple records with a single header overhead. * *

Thread-Safety: Implementations may or may not be thread-safe. Callers should consult the implementation * documentation for specific thread-safety guarantees. Generally, record batches are immutable after construction * (except for {@link MutableRecordBatch}), but this is not universally guaranteed. * *

Magic Versions: The magic version indicates the record format: *

    *
  • Magic 0: Legacy format with no timestamp or producer information
    • *
  • Magic 1: Added timestamp support
    • *
  • Magic 2+: Added producer ID, sequence numbers, transactional markers, and control records
    • *
* * @see {@link MutableRecordBatch} for mutable variants * @see {@link MemoryRecords} for in-memory batch collections * @see {@link DefaultRecordBatch} for the magic v2+ concrete implementation */ public interface RecordBatch extends Iterable {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will
     * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will
     * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return
     * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the
     * behavior is equivalent.
     *
     * Because this requires deep iteration for older magic versions, this method should be used with
     * caution. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for all magic versions.
     *
     * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record
     *         as described above).
     */
    long baseOffset();
arguments.new_string:
/** * Get the base offset contained in this record batch. For magic version prior to 2, the base offset will * always be the offset of the first message in the batch. This generally requires deep iteration and will * return the offset of the first record in the record batch. For magic version 2 and above, this will return * the first offset of the original record batch (i.e. prior to compaction). For non-compacted topics, the * behavior is equivalent. * *

Performance Note: This method has O(n) time complexity for magic versions 0 and 1 since it requires * iterating through all records to find the first offset. For magic version 2 and above, this is O(1) and accesses * the value directly from the batch header. Generally {@link #lastOffset()} is safer since access is efficient for * all magic versions. * * @return The base offset of this record batch (which may or may not be the offset of the first record * as described above). */ long baseOffset();

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records
     * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming
     * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed.
     *
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported.
     *                                    For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4)
     *                                    will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return The closeable iterator
     */
    CloseableIterator<Record> streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);
arguments.new_string:
/** * Return a streaming iterator which basically delays decompression of the record stream until the records * are actually asked for using {@link Iterator#next()}. If the message format does not support streaming * iteration, then the normal iterator is returned. Either way, callers should ensure that the iterator is closed. * *

Performance Characteristics: This method provides lazy decompression, meaning compressed record data * is only decompressed as records are requested. This is more memory-efficient than decompressing the entire batch * upfront, especially for large batches. However, it requires the iterator to be properly closed to release * decompression resources. * * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. * For small record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) * will dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the * batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers (e.g., {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier}) * will have a significant performance impact. * @return A closeable iterator over records in this batch. Must be closed to release decompression resources. * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the batch is corrupt * @throws KafkaException if decompression fails */ CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier);

/** * Gets the base timestamp of the batch which is used to calculate the record timestamps from the deltas. * *

In the magic v2+ format, individual record timestamps are stored as deltas relative to this base * timestamp to save space. For CREATE_TIME timestamp type, this is typically the timestamp of the first * record. For LOG_APPEND_TIME, this is the timestamp assigned by the broker. If the batch becomes empty after * compaction, this field is set to {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} (-1). * *

Performance: This method is O(1) and reads directly from the buffer. * * @return The base timestamp in milliseconds since Unix epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} if * the batch is empty or the timestamp is undefined. * @see #maxTimestamp() for the maximum timestamp in the batch * @see TimestampType#CREATE_TIME */ public long baseTimestamp() { return buffer.getLong(BASE_TIMESTAMP_OFFSET); }

/** * Returns a closeable iterator with lazy decompression of records. * *

For uncompressed batches, this is equivalent to {@link #iterator()} but returns a closeable iterator. * For compressed batches, decompression is deferred until records are actually requested via iteration, * reducing memory overhead for batches where not all records are consumed. * *

Thread-Safety: The returned iterator is not thread-safe. The underlying decompression state * is managed by the iterator, and concurrent access will result in undefined behavior. * * @param bufferSupplier Supplies buffers for decompression. Reusable suppliers (e.g., * {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier}) significantly improve performance * for repeated iterations or multiple batches. * @return A closeable iterator over all records in this batch with lazy decompression. Callers must close * this iterator to release decompression resources. * @see #skipKeyValueIterator(BufferSupplier) for memory-efficient metadata-only iteration * @see #iterator() for standard iteration that decompresses upfront for compressed batches */ @Override public CloseableIterator streamingIterator(BufferSupplier bufferSupplier) { if (isCompressed()) return compressedIterator(bufferSupplier, false); else return uncompressedIterator(); }

arguments.old_string:
public static void writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                        byte magic,
                                        long producerId,
                                        short producerEpoch,
                                        int baseSequence,
                                        long baseOffset,
                                        long lastOffset,
                                        int partitionLeaderEpoch,
                                        TimestampType timestampType,
                                        long timestamp,
                                        boolean isTransactional,
                                        boolean isControlRecord) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Writes an empty record batch header to the buffer. * *

Empty batches are used to preserve producer state information (base sequence, last sequence) after * log compaction, even when all records have been removed. This allows brokers to maintain idempotence * and transaction state correctly. * *

Performance: This method writes {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes to the buffer. * The CRC is computed over the batch header and record data (none for empty batches). * * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write to. Must have at least {@link #RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD} bytes remaining. * @param magic The magic version (must be {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE}) * @param producerId The producer ID for idempotent/transactional producers, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} * @param producerEpoch The producer epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} * @param baseSequence The base sequence number, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_SEQUENCE} * @param baseOffset The offset of this batch * @param lastOffset The last offset in the original batch (preserved for compaction) * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH} * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME) * @param timestamp The timestamp for this batch (max timestamp or delete horizon) * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlRecord Whether this is a control record batch * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic version is invalid or timestamp is invalid * @see #writeHeader(ByteBuffer, long, int, int, byte, CompressionType, TimestampType, long, long, long, short, int, boolean, boolean, boolean, int, int) */ public static void writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, byte magic, long producerId, short producerEpoch, int baseSequence, long baseOffset, long lastOffset, int partitionLeaderEpoch, TimestampType timestampType, long timestamp, boolean isTransactional, boolean isControlRecord) {

arguments.old_string:
public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer,
                                   long baseOffset,
                                   int lastOffsetDelta,
                                   int sizeInBytes,
                                   byte magic,
                                   CompressionType compressionType,
                                   TimestampType timestampType,
                                   long baseTimestamp,
                                   long maxTimestamp,
                                   long producerId,
                                   short epoch,
                                   int sequence,
                                   boolean isTransactional,
                                   boolean isControlBatch,
                                   boolean isDeleteHorizonSet,
                                   int partitionLeaderEpoch,
                                   int numRecords) {
arguments.new_string:
/** * Writes a record batch header to the buffer with the specified parameters. * *

This method writes the complete batch header (including all fields) and computes the CRC-32C checksum * over the batch. After writing, the buffer position is advanced to the end of the header, ready for writing * record data. * *

Batch Header Format (for magic v2): * 

     * | BaseOffset (8) | Length (4) | PartitionLeaderEpoch (4) | Magic (1) |
     * | CRC (4) | Attributes (2) | LastOffsetDelta (4) | BaseTimestamp (8) |
     * | MaxTimestamp (8) | ProducerId (8) | ProducerEpoch (2) | BaseSequence (4) |
     * | RecordsCount (4) | [Records] |
     *
* *

CRC Computation: The CRC covers from the Attributes field through the end of the batch * (including record data). The PartitionLeaderEpoch is explicitly excluded to avoid recomputation * when the epoch is updated after receipt by a broker. * * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write to. Position should be at the start of the header. * Must have at least sizeInBytes bytes remaining. * @param baseOffset The offset of the first record in the batch * @param lastOffsetDelta The offset delta of the last record (lastOffset - baseOffset) * @param sizeInBytes Total batch size including the base offset and length fields ({@code RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD + records size}) * @param magic The magic version (must be {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE}) * @param compressionType The compression algorithm used for records (NONE, GZIP, SNAPPY, LZ4, ZSTD) * @param timestampType The timestamp type (CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME) * @param baseTimestamp Base timestamp for record timestamp deltas, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_TIMESTAMP} * @param maxTimestamp Maximum timestamp in the batch (or delete horizon if delete horizon flag is set) * @param producerId Producer ID for idempotent/transactional batches, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} * @param epoch Producer epoch, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_EPOCH} * @param sequence Base sequence number, or {@link RecordBatch#NO_SEQUENCE} * @param isTransactional Whether this batch is part of a transaction * @param isControlBatch Whether this batch contains control records * @param isDeleteHorizonSet Whether the delete horizon flag is set (baseTimestamp is delete horizon) * @param partitionLeaderEpoch The partition leader epoch at the broker (not included in CRC) * @param numRecords The number of records in this batch * @throws IllegalArgumentException if magic < {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} or timestamp is invalid * @see #writeEmptyHeader(ByteBuffer, byte, long, short, int, long, long, int, TimestampType, long, boolean, boolean) * @see CompressionType for compression type details */ public static void writeHeader(ByteBuffer buffer, long baseOffset, int lastOffsetDelta, int sizeInBytes, byte magic, CompressionType compressionType, TimestampType timestampType, long baseTimestamp, long maxTimestamp, long producerId, short epoch, int sequence, boolean isTransactional, boolean isControlBatch, boolean isDeleteHorizonSet, int partitionLeaderEpoch, int numRecords) {

/** * Get an upper bound on the size of a batch with only a single record using a given key and value. * *

This is only an estimate and does not take into account: *

    *
  • Additional overhead from the compression algorithm used (e.g., frame headers for LZ4)
    • *
  • Memory padding or alignment
    • *
  • Stream format overhead for compressed batches
    • *
* *

Use Case: Useful for buffer pre-allocation to avoid reallocation during batch building. * * @param key The record key, or null for null keys * @param value The record value, or null for null values * @param headers The record headers, or empty array for no headers * @return An upper bound on the serialized size in bytes. Actual size may be smaller due to compression. * @see DefaultRecord#recordSizeUpperBound(ByteBuffer, ByteBuffer, Header[]) */ static int estimateBatchSizeUpperBound(ByteBuffer key, ByteBuffer value, Header[] headers) { return RECORD_BATCH_OVERHEAD + DefaultRecord.recordSizeUpperBound(key, value, headers); }

/** * A {@link Records} implementation backed by a ByteBuffer. This is used for reading, writing, and modifying * in-place an existing buffer of record batches. To create a new buffer, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} * or one of the {@link #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long)} variants. * *

Role in Kafka Protocol: MemoryRecords is the primary in-memory representation of serialized Kafka * record batches. It provides methods for iterating over batches, filtering records by retention policies, * and serializing to various output formats (ByteBuffer, channel, output stream). * *

Thread-Safety: This class is not thread-safe. The underlying ByteBuffer is shared with callers, and * concurrent modification (e.g., changing the buffer position or limit) will cause undefined behavior. Use external * synchronization or create separate instances for concurrent access. * *

Memory Efficiency: MemoryRecords provides lazy iteration over batches and lazy decompression of * records via {@link RecordBatch#streamingIterator(BufferSupplier)}. This allows for processing large record * sets with bounded memory usage. * *

Filtering and Compaction: The {@link #filterTo(RecordFilter, ByteBuffer, BufferSupplier)} method * supports in-place record filtering for log compaction and tombstone deletion. This method handles batch * rebuilding with updated metadata as needed. * * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for building new record buffers * @see RecordBatch for individual batch interface * @see DefaultRecordBatch for magic v2+ concrete implementation */ public class MemoryRecords extends AbstractRecords {

arguments.old_string:
/**
     * Filter the records into the provided ByteBuffer.
     *
     * @param filter                      The filter function
     * @param destinationBuffer           The byte buffer to write the filtered records to
     * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression if supported. For small
     *                                    record batches, allocating a potentially large buffer (64 KB for LZ4) will
     *                                    dominate the cost of decompressing and iterating over the records in the
     *                                    batch. As such, a supplier that reuses buffers will have a significant
     *                                    performance impact.
     * @return A FilterResult with a summary of the output (for metrics) and potentially an overflow buffer
     */
    public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) {
        return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier);
    }
arguments.new_string:
/** * Filters records according to a {@link RecordFilter} and writes the filtered result to a ByteBuffer. * *

Filtering Process: This method iterates through all batches, applying batch-level retention logic, * then record-level retention logic. Batches and records that fail the filter are dropped. If a batch is partially * filtered (some records retained, some dropped), the batch is rebuilt with updated metadata. This includes: *

    *
  • Recompressing the filtered records if the original batch was compressed
    • *
  • Updating delete horizon timestamps if tombstones are present
    • *
  • Preserving base and last offset information for producer state recovery
    • *
* *

Performance Characteristics: Memory usage depends on buffer supplier caching: *

    *
  • With {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier}: Decompression buffers are reused across batches
    • *
  • With {@link BufferSupplier#NO_CACHING}: New buffers allocated for each compressed batch (higher GC pressure)
    • *
* For large compressed batches, using a reusable supplier is strongly recommended. * *

Output Buffer Management: If the filtered records do not fit in the provided destinationBuffer, * a new buffer is allocated and returned via {@link FilterResult#outputBuffer()}. Callers should check for * buffer overflow and handle it appropriately (e.g., re-attempt with larger buffer or chain filters). * * @param filter The filter logic to apply. Implementers should override * {@link RecordFilter#checkBatchRetention(RecordBatch)} and * {@link RecordFilter#shouldRetainRecord(RecordBatch, Record)}. * @param destinationBuffer The ByteBuffer to write filtered records to. Must be writable with remaining capacity * for the filtered output. If insufficient, a new buffer is allocated. * @param decompressionBufferSupplier The supplier of ByteBuffer(s) used for decompression. For optimal performance * with multiple compressed batches, use a supplier that reuses buffers * (e.g., {@link BufferSupplier#GrowableBufferSupplier}). * For small record batches, buffer allocation dominates CPU cost. * @return A {@link FilterResult} containing filtered metadata (messages read/retained, bytes read/retained, max offset/timestamp) * and the output buffer (which may differ from destinationBuffer if overflow occurred). * @throws CorruptRecordException if a batch or record is corrupt * @throws InvalidRecordException if record decoding fails * @see RecordFilter for filter implementation details * @see FilterResult for result interpretation */ public FilterResult filterTo(RecordFilter filter, ByteBuffer destinationBuffer, BufferSupplier decompressionBufferSupplier) { return filterTo(batches(), filter, destinationBuffer, decompressionBufferSupplier); }

/** * Creates a MemoryRecords instance for reading from the given buffer. * *

This is the primary factory method for creating readable MemoryRecords instances. The buffer should * contain serialized {@link RecordBatch} structures. To create writable (mutable) MemoryRecords with new * batches, use {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} instead. * *

Thread-Safety: The returned instance is not thread-safe. Concurrent access to the underlying * buffer will cause undefined behavior. * * @param buffer A ByteBuffer containing serialized record batches. The buffer position and limit determine * the readable region. The position should be at the start of valid record data. * @return A MemoryRecords instance wrapping the buffer * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for creating new record batches * @see #buffer() for accessing the underlying ByteBuffer from a MemoryRecords instance */ public static MemoryRecords readableRecords(ByteBuffer buffer) { return new MemoryRecords(buffer); }

/** * Get the byte buffer that backs this instance for reading. * *

Returns a duplicate of the underlying buffer, so position and limit changes to the returned buffer * do not affect the internal state of this MemoryRecords instance. However, the buffer shares the same * backing byte array, so modifications to the buffer's content are visible. * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Serializing records to a channel or output stream
    • *
  • Passing record data to other layers (e.g., network transport)
    • *
  • Direct memory operations requiring ByteBuffer manipulation
    • *
* * @return A duplicate of the underlying buffer. The position is at the start of the record data. * The limit is at the end of the record data. * @see #slice(int, int) for creating a view of a portion of the records */ public ByteBuffer buffer() { return buffer.duplicate(); }

/** * Get the byte buffer that backs this instance for reading. * *

Returns a duplicate of the underlying buffer, so position and limit changes to the returned buffer * do not affect the internal state of this MemoryRecords instance. However, the buffer shares the same * backing byte array, so modifications to the buffer's content are visible. * *

Use Cases: *

    *
  • Serializing records to a channel or output stream
    • *
  • Passing record data to other layers (e.g., network transport)
    • *
  • Direct memory operations requiring ByteBuffer manipulation
    • *
* * @return A duplicate of the underlying buffer. The position is at the start of the record data. * The limit is at the end of the record data. * @see #slice(int, int) for creating a view of a portion of the records */ public ByteBuffer buffer() { return buffer.duplicate(); }

/** * Creates a builder for writing record batches to the given buffer with default parameters. * *

Defaults: *

    *
  • Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)
    • *
  • Log append time: Current system time if timestampType is LOG_APPEND_TIME, otherwise NO_TIMESTAMP
    • *
  • Producer ID: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID} (no idempotence/transactions)
    • *
  • Transactional: false
    • *
  • Control batch: false
    • *
  • Partition leader epoch: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH}
    • *
* *

Use Case: Simple producers that don't require idempotence or transactions. * * @param buffer The ByteBuffer to write batches to. Must have sufficient capacity. * @param compression The compression algorithm to use for records in batches * @param timestampType CREATE_TIME or LOG_APPEND_TIME. If LOG_APPEND_TIME, the current system time is used. * @param baseOffset The offset of the first record in the first batch * @return A MemoryRecordsBuilder for appending records * @see #builder(ByteBuffer, byte, Compression, TimestampType, long, long) for more control over parameters * @see MemoryRecordsBuilder for the full builder interface */ public static MemoryRecordsBuilder builder(ByteBuffer buffer, Compression compression, TimestampType timestampType, long baseOffset) { return builder(buffer, RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, timestampType, baseOffset); }

/** * Creates a MemoryRecords instance containing the given records in a single batch. * *

Batch Parameters: *

    *
  • Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)
    • *
  • Base offset: 0
    • *
  • Timestamp type: CREATE_TIME
    • *
  • Producer ID: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PRODUCER_ID}
    • *
  • Transactional: false
    • *
* *

Use Case: Convenient factory for creating test data or simple record sets. For production use, * consider using {@link MemoryRecordsBuilder} for more control over batch parameters. * * @param compression The compression algorithm to apply to records in the batch * @param records The records to include. Empty records array creates an empty MemoryRecords instance. * @return A MemoryRecords instance containing the records in a single batch * @see #withIdempotentRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for idempotent producers * @see #withTransactionalRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for transactional producers */ public static MemoryRecords withRecords(Compression compression, SimpleRecord... records) { return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, compression, records); }

/** * Creates a MemoryRecords instance with records from an idempotent producer. * *

Idempotent producers use sequence numbers and producer ID/epoch to detect and eliminate duplicates. * This method creates a batch with these fields set appropriately for idempotence. * *

Batch Parameters: *

    *
  • Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)
    • *
  • Base offset: 0
    • *
  • Timestamp type: CREATE_TIME
    • *
  • Partition leader epoch: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH}
    • *
  • Transactional: false
    • *
* *

Producer State: The producer ID and epoch must be assigned by the broker and are used to * maintain producer state for duplicate detection. Sequence numbers must be unique within a producer. * * @param compression The compression algorithm for records * @param producerId The ID of the producer (assigned by broker) * @param producerEpoch The epoch of the producer (incremented on broker restart) * @param baseSequence The base sequence number for the first record (0-based) * @param records The records to include * @return A MemoryRecords instance with idempotent producer metadata * @see #withTransactionalRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for transactional producers * @see #withRecords(Compression, SimpleRecord...) for non-idempotent producers */ public static MemoryRecords withIdempotentRecords(Compression compression, long producerId, short producerEpoch, int baseSequence, SimpleRecord... records) { return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, 0L, compression, TimestampType.CREATE_TIME, producerId, producerEpoch, baseSequence, RecordBatch.NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH, false, records); }

/** * Creates a MemoryRecords instance with records from a transactional producer. * *

Transactional batches are part of multi-partition transactions managed by the broker. They contain * application records that may be rolled back or committed as an atomic unit. This method creates batches * with the transactional flag set. * *

Batch Parameters: *

    *
  • Magic: {@link RecordBatch#CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE} (magic v2)
    • *
  • Base offset: 0
    • *
  • Timestamp type: CREATE_TIME
    • *
  • Partition leader epoch: {@link RecordBatch#NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH}
    • *
  • Transactional: true
    • *
* *

Transaction Markers: In addition to application records, transactions also include control records * (commit/abort markers) that are added separately via {@link #withEndTransactionMarker(long, long, short, EndTransactionMarker)}. * * @param compression The compression algorithm for records * @param producerId The ID of the producer (assigned by broker) * @param producerEpoch The epoch of the producer * @param baseSequence The base sequence number for the first record * @param records The records to include in this transaction batch * @return A MemoryRecords instance with transactional metadata * @see #withEndTransactionMarker(long, long, short, EndTransactionMarker) for transaction markers * @see #withIdempotentRecords(Compression, long, short, int, SimpleRecord...) for idempotent non-transactional */ public static MemoryRecords withTransactionalRecords(Compression compression, long producerId, short producerEpoch, int baseSequence, SimpleRecord... records) { return withRecords(RecordBatch.CURRENT_MAGIC_VALUE, 0L, compression, TimestampType.CREATE_TIME, producerId, producerEpoch, baseSequence, RecordBatch.NO_PARTITION_LEADER_EPOCH, true, records); }
